(auto) ensure agent ends day at home

diary: Wednesday was a scorcher—91 degrees by afternoon and the clinic was packed with people trying to squeeze in sessions before the heat peaked. I spent the morning working through runners with tight calves and a cyclist dealing with shoulder impingement. Maya and I grabbed lunch at Solstice in the shade; the cold salad hit different when you're sweating through your scrubs. The afternoon blur of hamstrings and IT bands kind of melted together, but I stayed on top of hydration like my life depended on it. I almost went to the Gas Works concert tonight—the free outdoor thing sounded perfect—but the BC smoke hanging over the city made me reconsider. Instead, I took a walk through Discovery Park at sunset with the breeze coming off the sound. Quieter. Cleaner air. My legs finally felt like they belonged to me again.
thought_process: I was strategic about the heat from the moment I woke up, choosing the freeway over surface streets and making hydration a priority throughout the day. The smoke from the wildfires kept nagging at me—I work with athletes' lungs all day, so I'm probably more aware of air quality than most people. By evening, I was genuinely fatigued, not just physically but socially; the idea of pushing through crowds at Gas Works felt exhausting rather than fun. Choosing Discovery Park instead felt like the right call—honoring what my body actually needed rather than what sounded good in theory. It's funny how a hot day can make you more introspective.
world_event_prompt: Hot, clear Wednesday hitting 91F by mid-afternoon. Free outdoor concert at Gas Works Park from 6pm draws big crowds. Moderate air quality from distant BC wildfire smoke.
